summary: Early Christian philosopher and Church Father from Hippo, associated with Confessions, The City of God, and On Christian Doctrine. Known for his views on original sin, grace, predestination, just war theory, and his conversion from Manichaeism to Christianity.

works.name: Confessions (Autobiography)
works.description: Augustine's spiritual autobiography recounting his conversion from Manichaeism to Christianity, heavily featuring his mother Monica. The text is one of the most frequently clued works in philosophy and literature, appearing across both categories. It describes his youthful sins, his intellectual journey through skepticism and Neoplatonism, and his dramatic conversion in a garden.

clues.clue: A child's voice chanting 'take up and read' prompts Augustine's conversion to Christianity in a garden
examples: While in a garden, its narrator hears a voice saying 'take up and read', prompting him to read Romans 13.
a child's voice chanting 'take up and read!' prompts this author's conversion to Catholicism.

clues.clue: Describes his conversion from Manichaeism to Christianity
examples: name this theologian who wrote The City of God and Confessions
this autobiography of St. Augustine

clues.clue: Recounts stealing pears with friends and feeling ashamed; threw most to the hogs
examples: This author's mother Monica arranges a marriage for him in a book that recounts him stealing pears.
The author of this text recalls robbing a pear tree with his friends and is ashamed to have thrown most of the food to the hogs.

clues.clue: His mother Monica features prominently; her death is described; she arranges a marriage for him

examples: the protagonist of a book by this author reads in a garden with his friend Alypius after he requests to 'make me chaste, but not yet.'

clues.clue: Wittgenstein's Philosophical Investigations opens with a quote from the Confessions about language acquisition
examples: A quote from this book beginning 'I was no longer an infant that could not speak' opens Ludwig Wittgenstein's Philosophical Investigations.
this writer whose conceptualization of language as associating names to objects is taken as the starting point for Wittgenstein's Philosophical Investigations

clues.clue: Petrarch read the Confessions during his ascent of Mont Ventoux; the passage about 'mighty waves of the sea' provided an epiphany
examples: This writer's claim, 'men wonder at the heights of mountains, and the mighty waves of the sea...but they consider themselves not' provided Petrarch an epiphany during his ascent of Mont Ventoux.

clues.clue: Augustine appears in Petrarch's Secretum dialogues, chastising the author
examples: This person chastises a later author for forgetting his mortality in three dialogues from the book Secretum.

clues.clue: Book XI explores God's timeless eternity
examples: This philosopher and saint explored eternity in Book XI of his Confessions through considering God's timeless eternity.

examples: In this text, the narrator says he is captivated by stage plays which provide 'fuel' for his own fire of miseries.

clues.clue: In part nine, overjoyed to end his rhetoric professorship; ecstatic experience in Ostia
examples: In part nine of this text, the author describes being overjoyed to end his professorship of teaching rhetoric, after which he has an ecstatic experience in Ostia.

clues.clue: Praises Cicero's Hortensius as 'exhortation to philosophy'; origin of 'evil is privation of good' view
examples: This book, which praises the 'exhortation to philosophy' of Cicero's 'Hortensius,' describes the origin of its author's view that evil is but the 'privation of good'

works.name: The City of God
works.description: Augustine's major theological and political work, written in response to the sack of Rome by Alaric in 410 AD. It frames human history as a conflict between the sinful 'earthly city' and the heavenly 'City of God,' and is the origin of the term 'just war.' The work also engages with pagan philosophy, including a polemic against Varro.

clues.clue: Frames history as conflict between earthly city and heavenly City of God
examples: A book by this thinker frames the history of humanity as a conflict between a sinful 'earthly city' and the title location.
He wrote of world history as a conflict between God and the Devil, and between the Earthly City and the title place

clues.clue: Written in response to Alaric's sack of Rome in 410 AD
examples: a book written as a response to Alaric's sack of Rome

clues.clue: Originated the term 'just war'
examples: The phrase 'just war' was first used in this book, which says that a 'wise man' should 'lament the necessity' of just war.
In City of God Augustine condones this action, which was examined further by Thomas Aquinas.

clues.clue: Augustine expressed skepticism towards the overall good of philosophy in this work
examples: Strauss also noted how this early Christian author expressed skepticism towards the overall good of philosophy in The City of God.

clues.clue: Contains a polemic against Marcus Terentius Varro's Antiquitates rerum divinarum
examples: One of his books contains a lengthy polemic against Marcus Terentius Varro's Antiquitates rerum divinarum.

clues.clue: Argues against Platonic natural theology
examples: This book, which was written after the sack of Rome in 410 AD, argues against Platonic natural theology.

clues.clue: Contains 'if I am mistaken, I exist' - an early cogito-like argument
examples: this philosopher and saint who anticipated Descartes's cogito argument by writing 'if I am mistaken, I exist' in his treatise The City of God

works.name: Original Sin, Grace, and Pelagius
works.description: Augustine's theology of original sin and divine grace was developed largely in opposition to Pelagius, who denied heritable original sin and believed humans could free themselves from sin through their own efforts. Augustine's views on predestination, concupiscence, and irresistible grace profoundly influenced Western Christian theology, including Calvinism and Jansenism.

clues.clue: Opposed Pelagius, who denied original sin and believed humans could free themselves from sin
examples: The theologian Pelagius was condemned for denying this thinker's conception of original sin.
Augustine accused this British-born theologian of heresy for his denials of heritable original sin

clues.clue: Claimed original sin was transmitted by concupiscence
examples: This thinker claimed that original sin was transmitted by concupiscence

clues.clue: Distinguished between 'prevenient' and 'operative' grace to justify predestination
examples: This thinker influenced Wesleyan-Arminian theology with his distinction between 'prevenient' and 'operative' grace

clues.clue: Argued that human capability to not sin was lost following the Fall of Adam
examples: This thinker argued that the human capability to not sin was lost following the Fall of Adam and emphasised the role of Divine Grace in salvation

clues.clue: Views developed into the Calvinist doctrine of irresistible grace
examples: Augustine's views developed into this Calvinist doctrine that the Holy Spirit must and does act, often suddenly, on each member of the predestined elect

clues.clue: Cornelius Jansen's posthumously-published magnum opus was a treatise on Augustine; Jansenism named after Augustine
examples: Cornelius Jansen's posthumously-published magnum opus was a treatise on this philosopher.
The primary text of Jansenist thought was named after this Bishop of Hippo

clues.clue: Michael Baius's theology was based on Augustine's
examples: This philosopher's theology was the basis for that of Michael Baius.

works.name: On Christian Doctrine
works.description: A four-book work on how to teach and interpret scripture. It divides the world into 'signs' and 'things,' outlines teaching styles (subdued, moderate, grand) inspired by Cicero, and quotes Ticonius's seven rules of interpretation. The work has been cited in discussions of rhetoric, religion as discipline, and semiotics.

clues.clue: Four-book work on how to teach the Scriptures (De doctrina christiana)
examples: This author of On Christian Doctrine and On the Trinity originated the term 'just war'
He wrote a four-book work on how to teach the Scriptures called De doctrina christiana.

clues.clue: Divides the world into 'signs' and 'things'; influenced by Cicero on teaching styles (subdued, moderate, grand)
examples: Inspired by Cicero, this thinker outlined 'subdued,' 'moderate,' and 'grand' teaching styles in a book on textual interpretation that divides the world into 'signs' and 'things.'

clues.clue: Quoted Ticonius's seven rules of interpretation
examples: This thinker quoted Ticonius's seven rules of interpretation in a work that argued for using signs to properly teach scripture.

clues.clue: Talal Asad and Timothy Fitzgerald cite it in defining religion as 'discipline'
examples: Asad argued that Christianity was understood as a 'discipline' prior to the concept of religion, citing a four-book work by this author

works.name: Evil as Privation of Good (Theodicy)
works.description: Augustine introduced to Christian philosophy the Neoplatonic idea that evil is merely the absence or 'privation' of good. This concept is a frequently clued aspect of his thought, appearing in both the Confessions and in his broader theological writings.

clues.clue: Defined evil as 'the privation of good' - introduced from Neoplatonism to Christian philosophy
examples: This thinker introduced to Christian philosophy the Neoplatonic idea that evil is merely the absence of good.
defined evil as 'the privation of good' in his theodicy

clues.clue: Non-Descartes thinker who asserted certainty of existence, knowledge, and love in an early cogito-like argument
examples: This non-Descartes thinker asserted the certainty of existence, knowledge, and love in an early cogito-like argument.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Common identifiers and biographical details that appear across clues: Bishop of Hippo, Church Father, probable Berber heritage, baptized alongside his son Adeodatus, died during a Vandal siege. Augustine is also associated with the Augustinian hypothesis (Matthew-first Gospel priority) and with the 'Athens and Jerusalem' dichotomy.

clues.clue: Bishop of Hippo; Church Father; author of Confessions and City of God
examples: For 10 points, name this theologian who wrote The City of God and Confessions.
name this author of City of God, whose conversion to Christianity is detailed in his Confessions

clues.clue: God created the world instantaneously rather than over six days (Literal Meaning of Genesis)
examples: This thinker claimed that God created the world instantaneously rather than over the course of six days in the work The Literal Meaning of Genesis.

clues.clue: Resolved omnipotence paradoxes by claiming God has power over everything in His will

clues.clue: Used Delphic maxim 'know thyself' to argue the mind is immaterial
examples: This thinker used the Delphic maxim 'know thyself' to argue that the mind exists, yet is immaterial, since it cannot know its own substance with certainty.

clues.clue: Described having a 'divided will' caused by bad habits, resolved by 'grace'
examples: This thinker described having a 'divided will' caused by bad habits that was resolved after he experienced 'grace.'

comprehensive_summary:
Saint Augustine of Hippo is one of the most frequently clued philosophers and theologians in quizbowl. He is primarily identified as a 5th-century Church Father, Bishop of Hippo, and author of the Confessions and The City of God.

The Confessions is Augustine's spiritual autobiography, recounting his journey from Manichaeism to Christianity. The most iconic scene is his conversion in a garden, prompted by a child's voice chanting 'take up and read,' which leads him to read Romans 13. Other major episodes include stealing pears as a youth (a symbol of his sinfulness), his relationship with his mother Monica (who arranges a marriage for him and whose death he mourns), his plea 'make me chaste, but not yet,' and his friendship with Alypius. The Confessions has significant literary afterlife: T.S. Eliot's The Waste Land quotes 'to Carthage then I came' and 'thou pluckest me out'; Wittgenstein's Philosophical Investigations opens with a passage about language acquisition from the Confessions; and Petrarch read it during his ascent of Mont Ventoux, finding the passage about 'mighty waves of the sea' to be an epiphany. Augustine also appears as a character in Petrarch's Secretum dialogues.

The City of God, written in response to Alaric's sack of Rome in 410, frames human history as a conflict between the sinful earthly city and the heavenly City of God. It originated the concept of 'just war,' which was later expanded by Thomas Aquinas. The work contains a polemic against Varro, argues against Platonic natural theology, and includes the early cogito-like argument 'if I am mistaken, I exist.'

Augustine's theology of original sin and divine grace was developed in opposition to Pelagius, who denied heritable original sin. Augustine argued that the human capacity to avoid sin was lost after the Fall of Adam, that original sin is transmitted through concupiscence, and that divine grace (distinguished into 'prevenient' and 'operative' forms) is necessary for salvation. These views influenced Calvinism (especially the doctrine of irresistible grace) and Jansenism (Cornelius Jansen's major work was a treatise on Augustine's thought).

Augustine introduced to Christian philosophy the Neoplatonic idea that evil is the 'privation of good.' He also wrote On Christian Doctrine, a four-book work on scriptural interpretation that divides the world into 'signs' and 'things' and outlines teaching styles influenced by Cicero. Additional clued facts include: he pioneered the argument that God created the world instantaneously, he used the Delphic maxim 'know thyself' to argue for the immateriality of the mind, he was of probable Berber heritage, he was baptized alongside his son Adeodatus, and he died during a Vandal siege of Hippo. The Augustinian hypothesis regarding Gospel priority (Matthew first) is also occasionally clued.
